stories.headline: Red flag warnings return Saturday as winds up to 75 mph threaten Front Range, mountain snow to follow
stories.summary:
Critical fire conditions returned to Colorado's Front Range and Eastern Plains on Saturday, with red flag warnings in effect from 11 a.m. to 9 p.m. for 25 counties including Denver, Boulder, Jefferson, Arapahoe and Douglas. Wind gusts up to 60 mph were forecast along the Interstate 25 corridor, with 70 mph possible in the Boulder and Jefferson County foothills, and humidity values as low as 8 percent.

The National Weather Service warned that fires would "uncontrollably spread and be very destructive" under the conditions. High wind warnings were also issued for the Sawatch and Sangre de Cristo mountain ranges through Sunday afternoon, with gusts to 75 mph expected to down trees, knock out power lines and create near-zero visibility from blowing dust.

A significant weather reversal is forecast to follow. Mountain snow is expected to begin Saturday afternoon, with winter weather advisories in effect from 6 p.m. Saturday through 9 a.m. Sunday for portions of Boulder, Clear Creek, Gilpin, Grand, Jackson, Larimer, Park and Summit counties. Up to eight inches of accumulation is forecast at higher elevations by Tuesday.

The Denver metro area could see light snow beginning around 1 a.m. Sunday, with temperatures plunging from a Saturday high of 76 degrees to a Sunday high that may not reach freezing and an overnight low of 16 degrees by Monday morning.

stories.headline: Update: Xcel Energy ends Northern Colorado power shutoff, begins restoration
stories.summary:
Xcel Energy announced Saturday that a preemptive power shutoff intended to reduce wildfire risk in Northern Colorado has ended and that restoration efforts are underway. The utility had shut off power in the region due to dangerous fire conditions driven by high winds and low humidity.

The shutoff followed widespread concern about wildfire danger across Boulder and Jefferson counties, where Xcel had warned customers of possible outages. The company did not immediately specify how many customers were affected or provide a timeline for full restoration.

The development comes as a red flag warning remains in effect across much of the Front Range through Saturday evening, with forecasters warning of extreme fire danger before an expected weather shift brings snow to the mountains and cooler temperatures to the Denver metro area.

stories.headline: Boater and dog dead after fishing boat capsizes on Lake Pueblo; survivor swims to shore
stories.summary:
One person and a dog were killed Friday after a fishing boat capsized on Lake Pueblo State Park, according to Colorado Parks and Wildlife. A survivor swam approximately 100 yards to shore and was assisted by a passing group of boaters who flagged down responding officers, but the second occupant of the vessel remained missing for hours.

Colorado Parks and Wildlife's maritime emergency response team recovered the boater's body at 9:23 p.m. Friday near Pedro's Point, where the boat capsized around 12:45 p.m. A dog's body was found underneath the overturned vessel. The Pueblo County coroner's office will identify the victim and determine the cause of death.

Officials noted the fishing boat was equipped with life jackets, but neither occupant was wearing one at the time of the accident. A helicopter crew and multiple first responders participated in the hours-long search before the body was located.

"Colorado Parks and Wildlife offers our condolences to the family and friends of the victim, and everyone involved in this tragic incident," said Lake Pueblo State Park Manager Joe Stadterman.

stories.headline: Solo backcountry skier self-triggered fatal avalanche near Vail Pass, state report finds
stories.summary:
A solo backcountry skier who died near Vail Pass on March 7 triggered the 400-foot-wide avalanche that swept him to his death, according to a new report from the Colorado Avalanche Information Center. The man accessed the Boss Basin area by snowmobile and was last seen heading toward the terrain at approximately 1:15 p.m., having already skied the slope once that day.

Search and rescue crews from Summit County Rescue Group located the man's snowmobile on a ridge at 4 a.m. Sunday after he was reported overdue by a family member. A thermal-imaging drone identified the avalanche and ski tracks leading into it, and an avalanche rescue dog found the man's body buried more than a foot below the surface shortly after a helicopter transported rescuers to the site.

Investigators noted the man was buried approximately three feet deep and said a companion rescue may have improved his chances of survival, though they could not determine that with certainty. Solo backcountry travelers have accounted for 23 percent of Colorado avalanche deaths over the past five seasons.

The Colorado Avalanche Information Center used the incident to reiterate that backcountry travel in groups, combined with safe travel protocols, provides greater resources for rescue if something goes wrong.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature advances credit card fee bill, abandons sex work decriminalization measure
stories.summary:
A Colorado Senate committee gave initial approval Thursday to legislation that would block credit card companies from charging processing fees on the sales tax portion of transactions. Senate Bill 134 passed the Senate Business, Labor and Technology Committee on a 3-2 party-line vote, with Democrats in favor, after a broader version of the proposal was rejected in 2025.

In a separate development, Sen. Nick Hinrichsen of Pueblo announced he would voluntarily kill Senate Bill 97, which sought to decriminalize sex work statewide, at its first committee hearing Wednesday. Hinrichsen said the bill lacked sufficient support on the Senate Judiciary Committee to advance and that proceeding to a full committee vote would not be productive.

Gov. Jared Polis' office privately told lawmakers that he will delay any decision on commuting former Mesa County Clerk Tina Peters' nine-year prison sentence until after the Colorado Court of Appeals issues a ruling in her case. The update came shortly before all 66 House and Senate Democrats sent Polis a letter urging him not to reduce the sentence.

The Colorado House also passed House Bill 1005, pro-union legislation that would make it easier for organized workers to negotiate with employers. The bill is a rerun of a measure Polis vetoed in 2025, and it faces a similar outcome, though all House Democrats again voted in support.

stories.headline: Boulder community rallies against potential closure of National Center for Atmospheric Research
stories.summary:
Nearly three months after the Trump administration announced plans to dismantle the National Center for Atmospheric Research in Boulder, Colorado residents, business leaders and former elected officials continue to push back against the closure. Gov. Jared Polis encouraged public comments in support of NCAR ahead of a Friday deadline, and the Boulder Chamber of Commerce called the facility the "backbone of our weather economy."

NCАR employs approximately 830 researchers whose work addresses climate change, hurricane intensity, sea level rise and other environmental questions. Former U.S. Rep. David Skaggs, whose district included NCAR, sent a letter to National Science Foundation leadership arguing the costs of closing the facility are enormous and urging decision-makers to conduct a cost-benefit analysis.

Jonathan Singer, senior director of policy programs at the Boulder Chamber of Commerce, warned that NCAR's closure would be permanent and could set off a chain reaction affecting other federally funded scientific institutions. The facility is described as part of a broader ecosystem of meteorology and climate science that Boulder has developed over decades.

NCАR operates as part of the National Science Foundation and is considered one of the world's leading atmospheric research institutions. Supporters argue its closure would eliminate expertise and infrastructure that cannot easily be rebuilt.

stories.headline: Denver metro firefighters deploy to South Dakota to help battle 7,000-acre Qury Fire
stories.summary:
Two crews of firefighters from the Denver metro area traveled to South Dakota on Friday to assist with containment efforts on the Qury Fire, a 7,000-acre wildfire burning southeast of Custer that remained at zero percent containment as of Saturday afternoon. The fire has exhibited extreme behavior including running fire, torching trees and spot fires igniting ahead of the main fire line.

West Metro Fire Rescue deployed three crew members and warned that strong winds Saturday would further hinder firefighting efforts. North Metro Fire Rescue sent Engine Boss Craig Talbot and firefighters Alberto Lopez, Jeff Tipton and Shane Dougherty with a Type III Brush Engine; the crew is working with Division F on firing and holding operations to keep the fire away from homes.

Both departments described the deployment as part of the nationwide mutual aid system that sends local resources where they are needed most. Officials also noted that large wildfire assignments give firefighters experience with fast-moving fires that can be applied when protecting communities in Colorado.

stories.headline: Lakers beat Nuggets in overtime thriller as Dončić hits go-ahead shot, both stars post triple-doubles
stories.summary:
The Los Angeles Lakers defeated the Denver Nuggets in overtime Saturday at Crypto.com Arena in Los Angeles in a nationally televised game on ABC. Austin Reaves hit a game-tying shot late in regulation to force overtime, and Luka Dončić then hit the go-ahead basket in the extra period to seal the win for Los Angeles, giving the Lakers their fifth consecutive victory.

Dončić finished with 30 points, 11 rebounds and 13 assists, while Nikola Jokić recorded 24 points, 16 rebounds and 14 assists — both players posting triple-doubles in the contest. Reaves contributed 32 points, seven rebounds and six assists for the Lakers.

The loss is a setback for Denver as the Nuggets navigate the final stretch of the regular season in a competitive Western Conference playoff race. The game featured a dramatic sequence in overtime with multiple lead changes before Dončić's decisive shot with half a second remaining.

stories.headline: Avalanche visit Jets in Winnipeg, MacKinnon still chasing McDavid for scoring lead
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche traveled to Winnipeg on Saturday to face the Jets at Canada Life Centre, completing a two-game road swing after their 5-1 rout of the Seattle Kraken. Colorado entered the matchup holding a five-point lead over the Dallas Stars in the Western Conference standings with a game in hand.

Nathan MacKinnon remains second in NHL scoring with 108 points, trailing Edmonton's Connor McDavid by three points, though MacKinnon leads the league with 44 goals. Martin Nečas has reached 30 goals on the season, while Cale Makar is one goal away from a fourth consecutive 20-goal campaign.

Mackenzie Blackwood was expected to start in net for Colorado after a shaky outing earlier in the week. The game also featured the continued integration of defenseman Nick Blankenburg, acquired from Nashville, who made his debut Thursday and logged 9:31 of ice time. Ross Colton's status remained uncertain as he recovers from an upper-body injury sustained against Edmonton.

stories.headline: Broncos sign cornerback Darius McMillian to restricted free agent tender worth $5.767 million
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos cornerback Darius McMillian signed his one-year restricted free agent tender on Friday, locking him in at $5.767 million for the 2026 season. The deal represents a substantial raise for the 25-year-old, who earned $1.03 million in 2025 after joining the team as an undrafted free agent in 2022.

McMillian appeared in all 17 regular-season games last season, making three starts while recording 56 tackles, a career-high 4.0 sacks, nine pass breakups, two interceptions and two forced fumbles. He is currently the Broncos' starting slot cornerback.

The signing comes as Denver remains the only NFL team that has not added a player from outside its own organization during the current free agency period, drawing criticism from fans and media. Re-signing McMillian continues the team's approach of retaining its own players rather than pursuing outside free agents.

stories.headline: Denver architect lists custom-built Mediterranean villa on historic 7th Avenue for $5.4 million
stories.summary:
Denver architect Andrew Sumners has listed a custom-built, 7,400-square-foot Mediterranean-style villa at 1650 E. Seventh Ave. for $5.4 million, nine years after purchasing the previous structure on the site for $900,000. Sumners, who grew up two houses away and attended East High School, said he was motivated in part by a childhood desire to replace what he described as a yellow eyesore from 1953.

The seven-bedroom, seven-bath home features handcrafted materials including a custom iron front door, 8-foot blond mahogany interior doors and a custom garage door, all imported from Mexico, along with a New Hampshire slate roof, white oak floors and 10-foot coffered ceilings. The kitchen includes a La Cornue range and light leathered marble countertops.

Construction required close coordination with the Denver Landmark Preservation Commission, which reviews all changes in the East 7th Avenue Historic District and required full exterior approval before work could begin. Sumners said the process forced him to design from the outside in, fitting the interior program within a fixed exterior envelope.

Sumners has spent much of his career developing projects in Mexico and Denver, including the Townhouses at Cherry Creek North, and typically works on one or two projects at a time over multi-year timelines.

stories.headline: Alterra Mountain Co. CEO Jared Smith to step down at end of 2025-26 ski season
stories.summary:
Alterra Mountain Company CEO Jared Smith announced he will step down at the conclusion of the 2025-26 ski season, marking a leadership transition at the Denver-based company that owns and operates the Ikon Pass and 15 ski resorts across North America, including Colorado destinations such as Steamboat, Winter Park and Copper Mountain.

Alterra is one of the two dominant players in the North American ski industry alongside Vail Resorts, and Smith's departure comes during a period of continued growth and competition for destination skiers. The company has not announced a successor.

The timing of the announcement, mid-season, suggests a planned transition rather than an abrupt departure, though no specific reason for the change in leadership was disclosed.

joke.joke: I'm in shape. Unfortunately, that shape is a potato.

topStory.headline: Six Air Force Crew Members Identified After KC-135 Tanker Crashes in Western Iraq
topStory.summary:
A U.S. Air Force KC-135 Stratotanker refueling aircraft crashed in western Iraq on Thursday afternoon, killing all six crew members aboard. The Pentagon publicly identified the airmen on Saturday, and U.S. Central Command confirmed the crash was not caused by hostile fire or friendly fire, with the circumstances remaining under investigation.

The six airmen killed were Maj. John A. Klinner, 33, of Auburn, Alabama; Capt. Ariana G. Savino, 31, of Covington, Washington; Tech. Sgt. Ashley B. Pruitt, 34, of Bardstown, Kentucky; Capt. Seth R. Koval, 38, of Mooresville, Indiana; Capt. Curtis J. Angst, 30, of Wilmington, Ohio; and Tech. Sgt. Tyler H. Simmons, 28, of Columbus, Ohio. Klinner, Savino and Pruitt were assigned to the 6th Air Refueling Wing at MacDill Air Force Base in Florida and served with the 99th Air Refueling Squadron at Sumpter Smith Joint National Guard Base in Birmingham, Alabama; Koval, Angst and Simmons were assigned to the 121st Air Refueling Wing at Rickenbacker Air National Guard Base near Columbus, Ohio.

Savino, a 2017 graduate of Central Washington University and King County, Washington native, had earned her pilot's wings in 2025 after previously serving as a Combat Systems Officer. She had logged 348 combat hours, deployed twice in support of U.S. Central Command operations, and been awarded the Air Medal and the Air and Space Commendation Medal. Klinner, who served as chief of squadron standardizations and evaluations, had deployed four times since 2019, logging 181 combat or combat support hours, and left behind a wife and three young children.

The crash occurred during Operation Epic Fury, the Pentagon's name for ongoing U.S. military operations against Iran. The six deaths bring the total number of U.S. troops killed in connection with the conflict to 13, according to CNN. The incident follows a separate episode last week in which Kuwait mistakenly shot down three American F-15E Strike Eagles operating in support of the same mission.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called the crew "American heroes" at a Pentagon briefing Friday, stating the military would receive them at Dover Air Force Base. Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen. Dan Caine said their deaths were "a reminder of the true cost of the dedication and commitment of the joint force." Governors of Alabama and Ohio also issued statements mourning the loss of the airmen, as the investigation into the cause of the crash continues.
